description: This figure presents a hierarchical flowchart classifying Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steels into two primary categories based on manganese and carbon content, and correlating their composition with mechanical properties and applications. The left branch represents multiphase Fe–Mn–Al–C steels containing 5–12 wt.% Mn and ≤0.8 wt.% C, which exhibit a strength-ductility balance of 800–1200 MPa tensile strength and 30–60% elongation, finding applications in automotive structural components and reinforcements. The right branch depicts austenitic Fe–Mn–Al–C steels with >12 wt.% Mn and 0.8–1.2 wt.% C, displaying a broader strength range of 600–1500 MPa with elongation below 60%, suitable for automotive structural parts and military armor applications. The diagram visually establishes the composition-property-application relationships, demonstrating how increasing Mn and C content shifts the steel from multiphase (ferrite-austenite) to fully austenitic microstructures, thereby altering the strength-ductility trade-off and end-use suitability. All numerical values including composition ranges, mechanical property data, and application sectors are explicitly labeled within the flowchart boxes.
